pi_items.source: task.json description explicitly specifies 3 pages: Overview, Details, and Checks and Balances
USA.gov (https://www.usa.gov/branches-of-government) and whitehouse.gov confirm these are the three official branches of US government

Verified through multiple official sources: whitehouse.gov (legislative and executive branches), uscourts.gov (judicial branch), and usa.gov confirm: Congress is the legislative body composed of Senate and House of Representatives; President is head of executive branch; Supreme Court is highest court in judicial branch
Official term is 'Checks and Balances' - verified via USA.gov, bensguide.gpo.gov, and constitution.congress.gov

All examples verified: (1) Veto - President can veto legislation (archives.gov); (2) Impeach - House impeaches, Senate convicts; (3) Override - Congress overrides with 2/3 vote (archives.gov); (4) Appoint - President appoints federal judges (constitution.congress.gov); (5) Confirm - Senate confirms presidential appointments (senate.gov, constitution.congress.gov); (6) Judicial Review - Courts can declare laws unconstitutional per Marbury v. Madison (uscourts.gov, fjc.gov); (7) Unconstitutional - standard legal term in judicial review

task.json description states 'Create a visual diagram using shapes (rectangles/circles) and lines/arrows' but does not specify exact minimum. Verifier.py requires shape_count >= 6 for full credit. No external source provides this specific number.

privileged_info_summary: The task requires creating an ActivInspire flipchart with accurate civics content about the US Government. The Three Branches of Government are Legislative (Congress: Senate and House of Representatives), Executive (President), and Judicial (Supreme Court). The system of Checks and Balances allows each branch to limit the others, including: Presidential veto and Congressional override, Congressional impeachment power, Presidential appointment of judges with Senate confirmation, and Judicial review allowing courts to declare laws unconstitutional. All required terms and check examples are factually accurate representations of the actual U.S. government structure and have been verified against official government sources (USA.gov, whitehouse.gov, senate.gov, uscourts.gov, constitution.congress.gov).
